text: Which Google Cloud resource lets a network team own subnets and firewall rules in one project while application teams deploy workloads in their own projects against those subnets?
options: VPC Network Peering between each application project's VPC and a central transit VPC owned by the network team
Shared VPC (host project + service projects)
Cloud NAT configured in the network team's project to provide outbound internet access for the application projects
AWS Transit Gateway, attaching each application VPC to a central hub managed by the network team
explanation: Shared VPC is purpose-built for this split-ownership model. Peering connects equals; Cloud NAT is for outbound public traffic; Transit Gateway is AWS.

text: Your organization wants centralized control of network policies and shared subnets across many application projects. The networking team owns the network; application teams own their workloads. Which design matches Google's recommended foundation?
options: Give every application project its own VPC and connect them with VPC Network Peering through a central transit project
A host project with the Shared VPC plus folder-level hierarchical firewall policies; service projects attach for each application team
Place all teams in a single project, separate workloads by labels, and use IAM Conditions on resource.labels for tenant isolation
One VPC per application project plus a single Cloud Router instance shared across them via NCC hub-and-spoke
explanation: Shared VPC + folder-level hierarchical firewall policies is the security-foundations blueprint pattern. VPC Peering scales poorly (n*n) and doesn't centralize firewall rules. Single project violates blast-radius isolation and lets one team's IAM mistake hit everyone. NCC hub-and-spoke is for cross-VPC/on-prem connectivity, not for centralized firewall policy across application teams.

text: An enterprise migrating ~300 projects to GCP needs: (a) per-business-unit chargeback; (b) org-wide security guardrails with BU-level customization; (c) centralized network management; (d) blast-radius isolation per BU; (e) self-service project provisioning that automatically inherits guardrails. Which foundation BEST satisfies ALL five?
options: Flat list of projects directly under the org node, IAM granted per project, one shared billing account, and no folder hierarchy
Folder hierarchy (Bootstrap, Common, BU → env → projects), Shared VPC, org policies with folder overrides, per-BU billing subaccounts, Project Factory
One mega-project per BU with workloads separated by labels and per-label IAM Conditions for blast-radius isolation across teams
A separate Google Cloud Organization per BU with org-policy syndication and Shared VPC peering for centralized network management
explanation: This is the security-foundations blueprint: folders enable inherited org policies and BU isolation; Shared VPC centralizes networking; per-BU billing sub-accounts enable chargeback; a Project Factory delivers self-service with guardrails baked in. Flat projects defeat inheritance/scale; one project per BU has no isolation; separate Orgs fragment governance.

text: You manage GCP infrastructure with Terraform from a CI pipeline. State is stored in a GCS bucket. Which configuration improves resilience against accidental loss of state?
options: Disable bucket versioning to keep state files small
Enable bucket versioning + uniform bucket-level access + lifecycle rules to retain N old versions
Store state in the same project as the workloads
Use local backend on the CI runner and upload to GCS at the end
explanation: Versioning + UBLA + a retention lifecycle is the standard hardening for TF state buckets.

text: An engineer added a firewall rule via the Cloud Console, bypassing your Terraform pipeline. What is the most appropriate way to detect AND reconcile this drift going forward?
options: Schedule a weekly manual audit of the Console for each project; require engineers to file a ticket when they make a manual change so the platform team can backfill it into Terraform
Run terraform plan in CI on a schedule and on PRs; investigate any non-empty plan, either import to keep the change or apply to remove it, and restrict Console write access via IAM
Delete and recreate the project from scratch using the Terraform module so any out-of-band changes are dropped, accepting the brief outage as the cost of consistency
Disable the Compute Engine firewall API at the org level and require all firewall changes to flow through a custom Cloud Function gated by Cloud Build approvals
explanation: Periodic terraform plan in CI is the standard drift-detection mechanism; once detected you decide to import (keep) or apply (remove), and IAM restrictions prevent recurrence. Manual audits don't scale and rely on humans self-reporting. Recreating projects is destructive and loses unrelated state. Disabling the firewall API breaks every legitimate workflow and is not how Org Policy or Console access should be constrained.

text: A platform team wants to provide self-service infrastructure for application teams via a curated catalog (e.g. 'GKE cluster', 'Cloud SQL instance') with guardrails enforced. Which approach aligns with Google's platform-engineering recommendation?
options: Give application teams Owner role on their own projects and let them write Terraform from scratch
Build a service catalog using Service Catalog and/or Config Controller with curated Terraform/Config Connector blueprints
Email the platform team to provision each resource manually
Use only Cloud Console click-ops
explanation: Curated, opinionated blueprints exposed via Service Catalog or Config Controller (KRM/Config Connector) is the platform-engineering pattern Google recommends.

text: Your CI workflow needs to authenticate to Google Cloud from GitHub Actions without storing a long-lived service-account key as a GitHub secret. What is the recommended pattern?
options: Use a JSON service-account key stored in a GitHub encrypted secret
Use Workload Identity Federation: GitHub OIDC token → Google Cloud short-lived credential via google-github-actions/auth
Embed a refresh token in the workflow file
Use gcloud user credentials interactively
explanation: Workload Identity Federation is the keyless pattern recommended by Google and used by this repo's bootstrap.

text: You want to prevent secrets (API keys, private keys) from ever reaching Artifact Registry by catching them at PR time. Which control is most effective?
options: Run an Artifact Analysis vulnerability scan on every pushed image and delete it from Artifact Registry if a credential pattern is found in the layer manifest
Add a secret-scanner step (gitleaks/trufflehog) to the Cloud Build PR pipeline that runs against the diff and fails the build on detection before the image is built
Rely on a documented PR review checklist that asks reviewers to grep for AWS_, GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS, and BEGIN PRIVATE KEY in the changed files
Encrypt the entire source repository at rest with a CMEK so any leaked credentials are protected by Cloud KMS access controls in production
explanation: Catching the secret in the PR pipeline stops it before it ever lands in Git history or an image. Post-push image scanning is reactive — the secret is already in Git and possibly published. Reviewer checklists are unreliable for high-volume repos. CMEK on the repo encrypts data at rest but does nothing to prevent committing plaintext secrets that any read-permitted user can see.

text: A team's CI builds for many GCP projects. Requirements: (1) no long-lived service-account keys anywhere; (2) per-environment principle of least privilege (dev pipeline can't deploy to prod); (3) auditable mapping from a specific GitHub Actions workflow run to the GCP credential it used; (4) cross-org reuse (multiple GitHub orgs). Which design BEST meets all four?
options: One JSON service-account key per environment stored as a GitHub org secret, rotated quarterly via a scheduled workflow
A WIF pool with attribute conditions on `repository`/`workflow`/`environment` mapped to per-env service accounts with narrow IAM; OIDC subject logged
Per-developer personal access tokens stored in repository secrets and used by the workflow to call gcloud commands directly each run
Disable WIF and use `gcloud auth login` interactively at the start of each CI job, sharing a bot Google account across all pipelines
explanation: WIF attribute conditions on `repository`, `workflow`, and `environment` allow fine-grained per-env mappings without keys; OIDC subjects appear in Cloud Audit Logs for traceability; pools can accept multiple GitHub orgs as providers. Stored SA keys and PATs both violate the 'no long-lived key' rule.

text: Which Google Cloud feature continuously syncs Kubernetes manifests from a Git repository to one or more GKE clusters and reconciles drift back to the desired state?
options: Cloud Deploy, the managed progressive-delivery service that orchestrates promoted releases through staged rollout phases
Config Sync (part of Anthos Config Management)
Cloud Build triggers that run a pipeline on each push to apply the manifests once and exit without further reconciliation
Artifact Registry repositories that store container images and Helm charts but do not apply them to any cluster
explanation: Config Sync is the GitOps controller for GKE/fleets. Cloud Deploy orchestrates progressive delivery (different concern). Build triggers run pipelines; Artifact Registry stores artifacts.

text: You manage many GKE clusters across an enterprise and want consistent policies, multi-cluster service discovery, and centralized config management. Which feature should you adopt?
options: GKE fleets with Config Sync and Policy Controller
A single mega-cluster spanning all teams
Manual kubectl apply from a jumphost
ArgoCD instances per cluster with no central control plane
explanation: GKE fleets are the unit of multi-cluster management; Config Sync (GitOps) and Policy Controller (OPA Gatekeeper) deliver centralized config and policy.

text: A platform team manages 30 GKE clusters across regions and tenants. Requirements: (1) one source of truth for cluster + namespace configs (GitOps); (2) prevent non-compliant resources at admission (no privileged pods, required labels); (3) per-team namespaces with quotas; (4) centralized policy reporting across the fleet. Which combination is BEST?
options: kubectl apply from each engineer's laptop, with quarterly manual config audits and a shared spreadsheet tracking per-cluster drift
GKE fleet with Config Sync (root + namespace repos), Policy Controller constraints, per-tenant ResourceQuotas, and the fleet posture dashboard
Helm-only deployments per cluster with no admission controls; rely on PR review of charts and post-deploy reconciliation jobs for drift
ArgoCD instance per cluster with no central control plane; per-team repos and a custom Prometheus exporter for cross-cluster posture metrics
explanation: Config Sync = GitOps source of truth; Policy Controller = OPA Gatekeeper for admission constraints; ResourceQuotas isolate tenants; the fleet Policy Controller dashboard gives unified posture. The other options each miss admission control or central reporting.

text: Which deployment strategy gradually shifts a small percentage of traffic to a new revision, monitors health, then progresses to full traffic — minimizing blast radius?
options: Recreate
Canary
Big-bang
AWS CodeDeploy LinearAllAtOnce
explanation: Canary releases progressively shift traffic with health checks at each step. Recreate causes downtime; big-bang has maximum blast radius; the AWS option is not a GCP service.

text: You need to roll out a new Cloud Run revision to 10% of traffic, watch SLO health for 30 minutes, then progress to 100%. Which Google Cloud service orchestrates this canary release?
options: Cloud Build with a custom shell step calling gcloud run services update-traffic on a timer between phases
A Cloud Deploy delivery pipeline configured with a canary strategy and a verify phase that queries the SLO
Cloud Run traffic splitting set manually in the console between revisions, with operators watching SLOs by hand
GKE rolling updates with PodDisruptionBudgets, fronted by a global Application Load Balancer pointing at the Cloud Run service
explanation: Cloud Deploy is the release orchestration product; canary strategy declaratively defines phases (e.g. 10% → 100%) with verify jobs between them. Cloud Build can drive deploys but isn't a release orchestrator with native canary phases. Manual traffic splitting + manual watching has no automation or audit trail. Rolling updates apply to GKE, not Cloud Run, and an LB in front of Cloud Run doesn't change the deployment unit.

text: A team rolls out a new GKE service with these requirements: (1) progressive delivery 5% → 25% → 100% with a 30-minute soak at each step; (2) automatic abort if SLO error budget burn rate exceeds 5×; (3) reproducible deploy artifacts with attestations; (4) one approval gate before prod. Which Google Cloud design BEST fits?
options: kubectl apply with manual percentage edits between 5/25/100 phases; pager on 5xx spikes; rollback by re-applying the prior manifest from Git
Cloud Deploy canary (5/25/100); `verify` jobs query SLO burn rate to auto-abort; Binary Authorization attestor on the image; promotion approval to prod
Blue/green with two parallel stacks and a manual cutover at the global ALB; no automated health gating between blue and green deployments
Recreate strategy: drain the prior version then deploy the new one across all replicas at once, with a 5-minute observation window before scaling
explanation: Cloud Deploy canary supports phased percentages; `verify` jobs can call Monitoring and fail to abort the rollout; Binary Authorization gives the attestation requirement; promotion approvals satisfy the manual gate. kubectl/recreate/blue-green-without-gating each miss multiple requirements.

text: Which deployment pattern is characterized by maintaining two full production environments and switching all traffic from one to the other in a single cutover, with the prior environment kept warm for instant rollback?
options: Canary
Blue/green
Rolling update
Recreate
explanation: Blue/green keeps two parallel stacks and cuts over via a router/LB change. Canary shifts a small percentage gradually; rolling does instance-by-instance; recreate causes downtime.

text: You're rolling out a backwards-incompatible API change. Which deployment pattern lets you have both old and new versions running side-by-side and shift users gradually with the ability to cut back instantly?
options: Recreate strategy
Blue/green deployment with a router/load-balancer-controlled cutover
In-place update
Big-bang deploy
explanation: Blue/green keeps both stacks fully provisioned. The cutover is a router config change, and rollback is the inverse change — instant.

text: A backwards-incompatible API v2 must be released alongside v1 for 60 days. Constraints: (1) shared underlying database; (2) zero downtime; (3) per-tenant feature-flag opt-in to v2; (4) ability to dark-launch v2 read paths to all tenants for shadow comparison before any tenant opts in; (5) instant kill-switch on v2. Which strategy BEST fits?
options: Big-bang cutover during a 4-hour maintenance window, with a documented rollback plan and a static read-only mode shown to users while v2 deploys
v1 and v2 behind one ALB; per-tenant feature flags for opt-in; mirror reads to v2 for shadow comparison; expand–contract DB migration; flag kill-switch
Replace v1 with v2 atomically across all tenants and rely on Cloud Deploy rollback if any tenant reports breakage in the first hour after cutover
Deploy v2 only to a separate region and use weighted DNS round-robin to shift tenant traffic from v1 to v2 over the 60-day coexistence window
explanation: Expand–contract makes the shared DB safe; ALB + feature flags handle tenant opt-in and kill-switch; shadow traffic enables comparison without user impact. The other options either cause downtime, lack the kill-switch, or break sticky sessions.

text: Which Google Cloud service automatically starts a CI pipeline when commits land on a specific branch of a connected source repository?
options: Cloud Scheduler
Cloud Build triggers
Cloud Run jobs
Eventarc only
explanation: Cloud Build triggers connect to GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket/Cloud Source and start a build on configured branch/tag patterns. Cloud Scheduler runs cron, not VCS events; Cloud Run jobs are batch tasks; Eventarc routes events but doesn't define the build itself.

text: Your team wants integrated, opinionated CI/CD on GCP that builds container images, runs tests, scans them for vulnerabilities, and stores artifacts ready for Cloud Deploy to roll out. Which pair fits best?
options: Cloud Build (CI: build/test/scan/push) + Artifact Registry (storage) + Cloud Deploy (CD)
Cloud Functions only, with each pipeline stage implemented as an HTTP-triggered function chained together via Pub/Sub topics
Self-managed Tekton pipelines on a GKE cluster pushing finished images to the deprecated Container Registry product
Cloud Workstations as the build environment, with developers manually invoking gcloud builds submit before each release
explanation: Cloud Build → Artifact Registry → Cloud Deploy is the canonical native trio. Container Registry is deprecated.

doc_links.title: Cloud Build + AR + Cloud Deploy reference

text: A regulated team needs CI that: (1) builds in an isolated, ephemeral worker (no shared mutable state); (2) signs every artifact with SLSA L3-grade provenance; (3) blocks builds that pull from non-allowlisted base images; (4) integrates with Artifact Registry remote repos to proxy upstream packages; (5) zero long-lived credentials. Which Google Cloud setup BEST satisfies all five?
options: Self-hosted Jenkins on a long-running VM with shared workspaces, secrets in Vault, and post-build SLSA attestation generation by a custom plugin
Cloud Build private pools (ephemeral); SLSA provenance enabled; allowlist build step for base images; Artifact Registry remote repos; WIF for external auth
GitHub-hosted runners with stored service-account JSON keys rotated weekly, base-image allowlist enforced via PR review, and Cosign image signing
Bash scripts run on developers' laptops with signed commits required, plus post-merge re-runs in Cloud Build to attach SLSA provenance retroactively
explanation: Cloud Build private pools give ephemeral isolated workers; SLSA provenance is built-in; Artifact Registry remote repos proxy upstreams under your control; an allowlist check fails non-compliant builds; WIF removes long-lived keys. The other options violate at least one of the five constraints.

text: Your security policy requires that only container images signed by your CI's attestor can be deployed to GKE production clusters. Which Google Cloud service enforces this at admission time?
options: Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning, which scans pushed images for CVEs and reports findings to Security Command Center
Binary Authorization with an attestor and a cluster policy that requires the attestation
Cloud Armor edge security policies with managed WAF rules to block malicious traffic before it reaches the cluster
VPC Service Controls perimeters around the GKE control-plane API to prevent data exfiltration to outside projects
explanation: Binary Authorization is the admission controller that enforces signed-image requirements at deploy time on GKE and Cloud Run.

text: Which framework defines hierarchical assurance levels for software supply chain integrity, and which Google Cloud product helps you produce attestations to satisfy higher levels?
options: SLSA framework; Cloud Build emits SLSA build provenance and Binary Authorization gates deploy on attestors signed against that provenance
SOC 2 framework; Security Command Center collects evidence and emits attestations for deployments via a Cloud Run admission webhook
PCI DSS framework; Cloud Armor's adaptive protection emits per-build attestations consumed by GKE Policy Controller
ISO 27001 framework; Cloud KMS signs each container image at build time and the signature is verified by Artifact Registry on pull
explanation: SLSA is the supply-chain integrity framework; Cloud Build emits SLSA-compliant build provenance, and Binary Authorization attestors gate deployment on it. SOC 2/PCI DSS/ISO 27001 are general assurance/compliance frameworks that don't define supply-chain integrity levels, and the products listed (SCC, Cloud Armor, KMS) don't produce build provenance.

text: A regulated team must guarantee that production GKE only runs images that are: (1) built by their Cloud Build pipeline; (2) have SLSA-compliant provenance; (3) have NO critical CVEs at deploy time; (4) progress through dev → staging → prod with manual approval and full audit trail. Which combination meets ALL four?
options: Cloud Build provenance; AR vulnerability scanning; Binary Authorization requiring build + vuln attestors; Cloud Deploy pipeline with audited promotion approvals
Manual `kubectl apply` after a two-reviewer code review of the manifest, with post-deploy CVE scanning of the running image in production
Cloud Build with provenance generation; rely on engineers to check the Artifact Registry vulnerability report before issuing the prod deploy command
Artifact Registry vulnerability scanning plus a scheduled Cloud Function that deletes images failing the CVE threshold from the production repo daily
explanation: Binary Authorization composes multiple required attestors (build provenance + vulnerability gate); Cloud Deploy provides structured promotion with approvals; Audit Logs record every promotion. Manual apply lacks gating and audit; CB-only or scan-only each miss enforcement at admission.

text: What is an SLI vs SLO vs SLA in SRE practice?
options: SLI = the measurement (e.g. % requests <300ms); SLO = the target (e.g. 99.9% over 28d); SLA = the contractual obligation with consequences
All three terms are interchangeable industry jargon for the same generic 'reliability number' tracked by an SRE team
SLI is purely an internal engineering metric that is never instrumented or measured against customer-facing performance
SLO is a customer-facing promise, SLI is for engineers, and SLA is reserved for executive board reporting and unrelated to budget
explanation: SLI = indicator; SLO = internal target; SLA = externally promised number with consequences. SLOs are typically tighter than SLAs.

text: A team has burned ~40% of their monthly 99.9% availability error budget in the first week due to two incidents. The next sprint is packed with risky feature work. Per SRE practice, what is the appropriate response?
options: Ignore the budget and ship the features anyway
Freeze risky feature work for the remainder of the period and prioritize reliability fixes; resume features when the budget recovers
Loosen the SLO to 99.5% so the budget appears healthier
Add more dashboards but no behavioral change
explanation: The whole point of an error budget is to create an explicit incentive to slow feature work when reliability suffers. Loosening the SLO is gaming the metric; ignoring or only adding dashboards changes nothing.

text: A team's SLO is 99.9% availability over 28 days. They observe a sustained burn rate of 6× and want an alert that fires fast enough to act before the budget is exhausted, but not so noisily that minor blips page on-call. What's the recommended approach?
options: Page on-call for every individual error response so the team is aware of any failure the moment it occurs in production
Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting (e.g. fast-burn 14.4× over 1h AND slow-burn 6× over 6h)
Skip real-time alerting and review the cumulative error budget once per week during the engineering operations meeting
Alert only when the 28-day budget is fully exhausted, then declare an incident and freeze releases until the next window
explanation: Multi-burn-rate alerting reduces false positives while still meeting time-to-detect goals. Cloud Monitoring SLOs implement this directly.

text: Which Google Cloud service runs jobs on a recurring cron schedule and can target HTTP endpoints, Pub/Sub, and App Engine?
options: Cloud Tasks
Cloud Scheduler
Cloud Workflows
Eventarc
explanation: Cloud Scheduler is the managed cron service. Cloud Tasks is for asynchronous task execution; Workflows orchestrates multi-step flows; Eventarc routes event-driven invocations.

text: You need to provision GPU capacity for a 6-hour training job at a specific time tomorrow, in a region where on-demand GPUs are frequently unavailable. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Have a Cloud Scheduler job retry on-demand provisioning every minute until the GPU request succeeds in the target region
Use Dynamic Workload Scheduler in calendar mode to request a reservation for the required GPUs at the desired start time
Provision a 1-year committed-use discount for the GPU SKU to guarantee capacity in that region for the one-time job
Rewrite the job to run on Spot GPU VMs with no checkpointing and accept restarts on preemption, since Spot quota is usually available
explanation: Dynamic Workload Scheduler is purpose-built for time-bound AI/HPC capacity — calendar mode for known windows, flex-start for queued jobs. Polling for on-demand wastes time and isn't a capacity guarantee. A 1-year CUD over-commits for a single 6-hour job. Spot without checkpoints will lose all progress on every preemption.

text: An ML platform must serve burst training jobs that need 8 H100 GPUs each, on-demand within 5 minutes, in a region where on-demand H100 capacity is scarce. Constraints: (1) jobs are not fault-tolerant (no Spot); (2) cost must beat 1-year CUD purchase; (3) capacity must be guaranteed for the next 8 weeks. Which approach BEST fits?
options: Buy 1-year committed-use discounts (CUDs) for 8 H100 GPUs in the target region and accept the over-commitment
Use Dynamic Workload Scheduler in flex-start mode for queued capacity, plus a future reservation for the 8-week window when capacity must be guaranteed
Submit each job to on-demand H100 capacity in the scarce region and retry with exponential backoff until allocation succeeds
Refactor every job to checkpoint after each step and run on Spot VMs across multiple zones to absorb preemptions
explanation: DWS flex-start queues for capacity efficiently for non-urgent jobs; future reservations explicitly guarantee capacity for a known window without committing for a year. CUD is too long; on-demand isn't reliable in scarce regions; Spot violates the no-fault-tolerance constraint.

text: An incident is in progress: a new release is causing elevated 5xx errors. The fastest mitigation that respects the SRE principle 'mitigate before diagnose' is:
options: Open a debugging session and grep logs
Roll back the release to the previous known-good revision and then investigate
Email all stakeholders and wait for an architect to weigh in
Disable monitoring to silence the alert
explanation: SRE practice prioritizes user-visible mitigation (rollback / drain / redirect / capacity) before root-cause analysis.

text: A new Cloud Run revision is deployed and 5xx errors spike. Which fastest-to-root-cause approach combines the right Google Cloud signals?
options: Attach Cloud Debugger snapshots to the running container and step through the request handler line by line
Group Cloud Monitoring error rate by revision, check Error Reporting for new exception groups, and filter Cloud Logging to the suspect revision with severity>=ERROR
Open a Cloud Support case and wait for the next Google Cloud Status Dashboard update before taking action
Run a synthetic load test against the previous known-good revision to confirm baseline traffic still succeeds
explanation: Grouping by revision in Monitoring isolates the offending version; Error Reporting clusters new exception types; revision-scoped log filtering finds the actual stack traces. The other options don't accelerate diagnosis.

text: A Sev-1 incident is in progress: 5xx errors are spiking after a deploy, but the on-call notices the new revision also depends on a freshly migrated database schema. Three hypotheses are open. Per SRE practice, which sequence is BEST?
options: Convene a war room of senior engineers, complete root-cause analysis on all three hypotheses, then take the first user-facing mitigation action

Mitigate first by rolling back to the last known-good revision (and reverting the DB migration if reversible), communicate status, then diagnose with logs/traces/metrics, then write a blameless postmortem
Page the entire SRE org and wait for consensus on the most likely hypothesis before changing anything in production
Scale Cloud Run min-instances and GKE replicas up by 5x and watch whether the elevated 5xx rate clears on its own
explanation: SRE: mitigate → communicate → diagnose → postmortem. Rollback (and reversible DB revert) is the standard mitigation. Convening RCA first prolongs user pain; scaling without diagnosis is cargo-cult.

text: After a Sev-1 incident, what is the SRE-recommended best practice for postmortems?
options: Identify the engineer whose change triggered the incident and follow the standard performance-management process
Write a blameless postmortem focused on systemic causes and concrete action items, share it widely
Skip the postmortem if the immediate user-visible issue is already mitigated and the on-call rotation is back to normal
Keep the postmortem confidential to a small executive group so the customer-visible details are not shared too broadly
explanation: Blameless postmortems focus on the system, not individuals, producing actionable improvements.

text: Which postmortem action item is MOST likely to prevent recurrence of a class of incidents (vs. only the specific incident)?
options: Add a runbook entry describing the manual mitigation
Add an automated guardrail in CI/CD or platform code that makes the failure mode impossible (or detected pre-prod)
Send a company-wide email about the incident
Increase on-call rotation size
explanation: Systemic, automation-based action items (CI checks, admission policies, platform invariants) prevent the entire class of failure. Runbooks help react, emails inform, larger rotations spread load — none address the root systemic gap.

text: After three Sev-1s in 30 days from the same root cause class (a missing pre-deploy schema check), which set of action items BEST follows SRE postmortem guidance?
options: Email a reminder to all engineers to be careful, plus a mandatory training module on database migration best practices for the org
Automated pre-deploy schema-compatibility check in CI; Cloud Deploy `verify` job runs the same check; SLO on schema incidents; 60-day postmortem follow-up
Forbid all schema changes for 90 days while the team builds a manual approval workflow for any future migrations across the organization
Add a runbook step the on-call must run before deploys, plus a Slack reminder bot that posts a checklist before each Cloud Deploy promotion
explanation: Systemic action items (pipeline check, verify job, tracked SLO, follow-up) prevent the entire class. Reminders and runbook-only steps depend on humans remembering at the wrong time; banning schema changes is impractical.

text: You need a Cloud Monitoring alerting policy that pages PagerDuty when CPU exceeds 80% for 5 minutes on a Compute Engine MIG. Which two pieces are required?
options: A metric-threshold condition (compute.googleapis.com/instance/cpu/utilization > 0.8 for 5m) AND a notification channel of type PagerDuty
A log-based metric only
A Cloud Function only
An Eventarc trigger only
explanation: Alerting policies are condition + notification channel. PagerDuty is a first-class notification channel type. The other items don't deliver alerting on their own.

text: You operate a hybrid environment: GKE on Google Cloud and Kubernetes clusters on-prem. You want a single, OpenTelemetry-compatible metrics backend with Prometheus query language. Which option fits?
options: Run a separate Prometheus + Grafana stack per cluster, manually federated
Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus, with collectors running in each cluster
Cloud Logging only — metrics aren't needed
Send all metrics to BigQuery via Pub/Sub
explanation: Managed Service for Prometheus is the GCP-native, PromQL-compatible metrics backend designed for hybrid environments.

text: A platform team wants metrics that: (1) use PromQL; (2) are queryable globally across many projects; (3) retain raw resolution for 6 weeks and downsampled for 24 months; (4) accept Prometheus remote_write from on-prem clusters too. Which design BEST fits?
options: Self-managed Prometheus + Thanos cluster on GKE with object-storage long-term blocks and a per-region Sidecar federation layer for global query
Managed Service for Prometheus with a multi-project metrics scope; managed collection in each cluster; remote_write from on-prem clusters
Cloud Logging with log-based metrics emitted by each app; PromQL via the legacy `external.googleapis.com` adapter and a custom exporter
Send all metrics to BigQuery via Pub/Sub and query with SQL; expose PromQL via a custom Cloud Run adapter that translates queries to SQL
explanation: GMP is PromQL-compatible, supports remote_write from on-prem, has long native retention, and metrics scopes provide cross-project query. Self-managed Thanos is expensive ops; Logging isn't a metrics store; raw BigQuery loses PromQL.

text: Your Cloud Logging bill has grown sharply. Investigation shows that ~80% of ingestion is from one verbose application's INFO logs, which are not used for alerting or audit. What is the most cost-effective mitigation?
options: Delete the logs after ingestion using a TTL policy
Add an exclusion filter in the _Default sink to drop those INFO logs before ingest
Switch the application to write logs to Cloud Storage manually
Move all workloads to a different region
explanation: Logging is billed primarily on ingestion volume. An exclusion filter on the _Default sink drops matching logs before the ingest meter runs.

text: Which Cloud Audit Logs category is enabled by default and free, vs. one that is off by default and incurs ingestion charges when enabled?
options: Admin Activity (default-on, free) vs. Data Access (default-off, billed)
Both are default-on and free
Both are default-off and billed
System Event (default-off) vs. Policy Denied (default-on)
explanation: Admin Activity audit logs are always enabled and free. Data Access audit logs are off by default and incur Logging ingestion costs when enabled.

text: An organization with 200 GCP projects across many tenants needs: (1) all Admin Activity + Data Access audit logs centralized for 7 years; (2) per-tenant application logs routed to per-tenant BigQuery datasets retained 90 days; (3) drop verbose DEBUG logs to control cost. Which design BEST meets all three?
options: Org-level aggregated sink to a 7-year Cloud Logging bucket for audit logs; per-tenant BigQuery sinks with 90-day partition expiry; `_Default` exclusions for DEBUG
One sink per project to a single org-wide BigQuery dataset with 7-year partition expiry; no per-tenant routing or `_Default` exclusions for DEBUG logs
Export all logs to one Cloud Storage bucket with 7-year retention; grep historical data with on-demand Dataproc Spark jobs and per-tenant prefix paths
Disable Cloud Logging; have every workload write JSON to stdout, scraped by Fluent Bit and shipped to a self-managed Loki cluster with per-tenant streams
explanation: Org-level aggregated sinks centralize audit data; project-level sinks with tenant filters give per-tenant BigQuery routing; partition expiry handles 90-day retention; exclusions on `_Default` drop DEBUG before billable ingest. The other designs miss tenant routing, cost control, or governance.

text: Which Google Cloud tool would you use to find Cloud Run request logs with HTTP status 500 from the `payment` service in the last hour?
options: Cloud Logging Log Explorer with resource.type=cloud_run_revision, service_name=payment, httpRequest.status=500, last 1 hour
Cloud Trace span explorer filtered to the payment service over the last hour with HTTP status overlays
Cloud Profiler heap and CPU snapshots for the payment service captured during the past hour
BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_PROJECT query for jobs that touched the payment dataset in the last hour
explanation: Log Explorer with the standard Cloud Run resource/labels filter is the direct path. Trace shows latency spans, Profiler shows CPU/memory profiles, and INFORMATION_SCHEMA queries BigQuery metadata.

text: You want a dashboard panel that shows p95 latency of a service grouped by route, using PromQL. Which combination supports this in Cloud Monitoring?
options: Use one of the built-in service dashboards — Cloud Monitoring panels accept PromQL only when paired with Cloud Trace as the metric source
Build a custom dashboard with a PromQL widget querying Managed Service for Prometheus metrics scraped from the service's /metrics endpoint
Export the metrics to Looker Studio first — native Cloud Monitoring dashboards only support MQL and the legacy filter UI, not PromQL
Use Cloud Profiler to compute p95 latency per route — Cloud Monitoring dashboards don't support latency histograms or PromQL syntax
explanation: Cloud Monitoring dashboards support PromQL widgets natively, especially when paired with Managed Service for Prometheus. PromQL is not gated on Cloud Trace, MQL is not the only supported language, and Cloud Profiler is for CPU/heap profiling — not latency dashboards.

text: A platform team wants a single dashboard that combines: (1) a PromQL p99 latency widget from GMP; (2) a Cloud Logging-based-metric error count; (3) an SLO error budget burn-down; (4) a custom log query as a table. Constraints: must be defined as code (reviewable in Git) and deployed via CI. Which approach BEST fits?
options: Click around the Cloud Console to compose the dashboard and screenshot it as the source of truth for review by the platform team during PRs
Define the dashboard as Cloud Monitoring JSON (or `google_monitoring_dashboard` Terraform) with PromQL/log-metric/SLO/logs widgets; deploy via CI
Export the data to Looker Studio with scheduled refreshes; mail dashboard PDFs to the team weekly and store the rendered report in version control
Run self-hosted Grafana on GKE with the Cloud Monitoring data source; manage dashboard JSON manually inside the Grafana UI and back up to Git nightly
explanation: Cloud Monitoring dashboards support PromQL widgets, log-based-metric widgets, SLO burn widgets, and logs panels — all expressible in JSON / Terraform for GitOps. The other options violate the as-code requirement.

text: Which Google Cloud product collects CPU and heap profiles continuously from production with negligible overhead?
options: Cloud Trace
Cloud Profiler
Cloud Monitoring uptime checks
BigQuery
explanation: Cloud Profiler is the always-on, low-overhead production profiler. Trace is for distributed request spans; uptime checks probe endpoints; BigQuery is analytics.

text: You need to debug a latency issue that spans Cloud Run, Pub/Sub, and Cloud SQL. Which Google Cloud product correlates spans across all three so you can see end-to-end timing?
options: Cloud Profiler, which samples CPU and heap usage from running services to surface hot functions and memory allocations
Cloud Trace (with OpenTelemetry instrumentation)
Cloud Logging alone, relying on log search and trace_id correlation without a dedicated distributed-tracing backend
Error Reporting, which groups exceptions and stack traces from your services but does not record per-request span timing
explanation: Cloud Trace receives OpenTelemetry-formatted spans from your services and stitches them into end-to-end traces.

text: A platform team needs unified observability across Go, Java, and Python services on GKE. Constraints: (1) only OpenTelemetry SDKs in apps (no vendor SDKs); (2) traces, metrics, AND logs must correlate by trace ID; (3) backend must be fully managed by Google; (4) no custom exporters in app code. Which design satisfies ALL four?
options: OpenTelemetry SDKs export OTLP to a self-managed Jaeger + Prometheus + ELK stack on GKE; trace_id correlated by a Fluentd log enricher pipeline
OTel SDKs send OTLP to an OTel Collector that exports traces to Cloud Trace, metrics to Managed Prometheus, and logs to Cloud Logging with trace_id
Datadog Agent on every node forwarding traces, metrics, and logs to Datadog SaaS via OTLP, with Datadog APM providing the trace_id correlation layer
Cloud Profiler as the single Google-managed backend for traces, metrics, and logs; OTel SDKs export directly via the Profiler agent's OTLP receiver
explanation: Apps stay pure-OTel via OTLP to a Collector that fans out to Google-managed backends; injecting trace_id into log entries enables cross-signal correlation. Self-managed stacks fail the 'fully managed' constraint; Datadog violates the OTel-SDK-only and managed-Google constraints; Profiler doesn't replace tracing/logging/metrics.

text: A team wants to surface the most recent commit SHA inside a Cloud Run revision so an operator can confirm what's deployed. Which is the simplest GCP-native pattern?
options: Hard-code the latest commit SHA into the application source and rebuild the container image on every deploy
Pass COMMIT_SHA as a Cloud Run environment variable at deploy time and surface it on a /version endpoint
Manually update an internal wiki page with the new SHA whenever a deploy completes successfully
Write the SHA to a Firestore document at runtime and have /version read it on every request
explanation: Env vars set at deploy time travel with the revision and can be exposed by `/version`. Hard-coding requires rebuilds; wikis drift; Firestore is over-engineering.

text: GKE pods are being OOMKilled in production but not in staging. The team's release process needs an automated guard before promoting to prod. Which control would catch this most directly?
options: Add a Cloud Deploy verify job that runs a representative load test against the staging Service and asserts container memory_working_set_bytes stays under the configured pod memory limit before promotion
Increase the staging cluster's node pool to use larger machine types so staging memory headroom matches production exactly
Disable the Vertical Pod Autoscaler in production to keep pod requests stable and prevent OOMKill thrashing from misconfigured recommendations
Add a HorizontalPodAutoscaler scaling on memory utilization with a target of 70% so prod automatically scales out under memory pressure
explanation: A Cloud Deploy verify job that load-tests against the staging working-set vs. configured pod limit catches the misconfiguration *before* promotion. Bigger staging nodes mask the problem (the pod limit is the constraint, not node capacity). Disabling VPA does nothing about an under-sized memory limit. HPA on memory adds replicas but each replica still has the same too-small limit — they all OOM.

text: On a GKE Standard cluster, pods request 1 CPU each but actual utilization averages 30%. Nodes report 80% CPU allocatable used but only 24% real CPU. Which change reduces cost while preserving reliability?
options: Manually edit each Deployment to drop CPU requests by 60%, then rely on the HorizontalPodAutoscaler to add replicas if real load increases
Run Vertical Pod Autoscaler in 'Off' (recommendation) mode to learn right-sized requests, then apply them (or use 'Auto' mode where it's safe to evict pods)
Switch the workloads from CPU requests to BestEffort QoS by removing requests entirely; the scheduler will pack pods more tightly
Enable cluster-autoscaler in Optimize-Utilization profile and reduce min nodes; the autoscaler will consolidate underused pods automatically
explanation: VPA recommendations close the gap between requested and used CPU, letting more pods fit per node so the cluster shrinks — reliable because the recommendations are based on observed usage. Manually slashing requests guesses the new value and can starve burst traffic. Removing requests (BestEffort) breaks scheduling guarantees and HPA. Cluster-autoscaler reacts to *node* underuse but cannot consolidate pods that themselves over-request — the requests are the bottleneck.

text: A GKE Standard cluster must support spiky workloads cost-effectively while meeting these constraints: (1) cold-start under 60 seconds for new pods; (2) no permanently overprovisioned headroom; (3) per-pod memory right-sizing without restarting pods; (4) automatic node-pool optimization across machine types. Which combination BEST fits?
options: Provision static node pools sized for peak traffic and have the platform team manually adjust them during quarterly capacity reviews
Cluster Autoscaler + HPA + VPA in Initial/Auto mode + Node Auto-Provisioning across machine shapes, with image streaming for fast pod start
Run all workloads on Spot VMs without an autoscaler and tolerate preemptions through aggressive pod-level retry budgets
Switch to GKE Autopilot but disable HPA and VPA so the platform team retains full control of pod resource sizing
explanation: CA + HPA + VPA + NAP cover horizontal, vertical, and node-shape elasticity; image streaming addresses cold-start. Static sizing wastes money; Spot-only risks the cold-start SLO; disabling HPA/VPA in Autopilot defeats the purpose.

text: Your stateless web tier on Compute Engine has steady 24/7 traffic. The team wants to maximize discount without commitment-management overhead. What applies automatically?
options: Spot VM pricing
Sustained-Use Discounts, applied automatically based on per-month usage
1-year committed-use discount, requires manual purchase
Free tier
explanation: Sustained-Use Discounts (SUDs) apply automatically to GCE/GKE for resources running a large fraction of the month, with no commitment.

text: A GKE Standard cluster has these workload patterns: 40% steady 24/7, 30% weekday business-hours, 30% unpredictable bursts. Which discount strategy minimizes cost without stranding commitments?
options: Buy resource-based 1-year CUDs sized to 100% of peak capacity in each region the cluster runs in
Size 1-year CUDs to the 40% always-on baseline; let business-hours load run on-demand (sustained-use discounts apply automatically); use Spot VMs for fault-tolerant burst workloads
Buy resource-based 3-year CUDs sized to the 70% baseline-plus-business-hours combined, accepting some over-commitment for the deeper discount
Use Spot VMs for the entire cluster; checkpoint workloads frequently and accept that the steady-state tier may restart on preemption
explanation: Sizing CUDs to the always-on baseline maximizes commitment value without stranding it; SUDs apply automatically to weekday on-demand usage; Spot is ideal for fault-tolerant bursts. Committing to peak (option A) strands ~60% of the commitment outside business hours. Three-year CUDs at 70% (option C) lock in capacity that the unpredictable burst tier won't consistently use. All-Spot (option D) breaks the steady 24/7 reliability requirement.

text: An org wants to maximize discounts across a large GCE fleet but avoid stranding commitments as workloads shift between projects. Constraints: (1) shareable across projects under a billing account; (2) flexibility across machine families; (3) lowest discount-management overhead. Which choice BEST fits?
options: Resource-based committed use discounts purchased per-project per-machine-type
Flexible (spend-based) committed use discounts at the billing-account level, automatically applied across projects and eligible families
Spot VMs only
Sustained-use discounts only
explanation: Flexible CUDs commit to a dollar amount of compute spend and apply across projects under the billing account and across eligible families — least likely to be stranded as workloads move. Resource-based CUDs are project- and shape-locked; Spot/SUD don't satisfy the commitment-discount goal at scale.

text: Which Google Cloud feature lets you set spending thresholds with email alerts (and optional Pub/Sub notifications) per project or billing account, without capping spend automatically?
options: Quotas
Budgets and budget alerts in Cloud Billing
Cloud Armor rate limits
Recommender API
explanation: Budgets emit alerts at configurable percentages of a target; pairing with Pub/Sub allows automated reactions. Quotas cap usage but don't track spend.

text: Your monthly GCP bill jumped 40%. You need to identify which projects, services, and SKUs drove the increase, with breakdowns by labels (cost-allocation tags). Which tool is the right starting point?
options: Cloud Asset Inventory exports of resource state into BigQuery, joined to billing labels manually for cost-by-resource analysis
Cloud Billing reports + BigQuery billing export, sliced by project, service, SKU, and labels
Security Command Center Premium findings filtered by project to identify misconfigured resources that may be inflating spend
Cloud Trace latency reports for the affected services, looking for slow spans that might indicate accidental resource usage
explanation: Cloud Billing reports give built-in slicing by project/service/SKU/label; the BigQuery billing export lets you query the full detailed cost dataset for ad-hoc breakdowns. Asset Inventory tracks resource state, not cost; SCC is security; Trace is latency.

text: A platform team is building a FinOps capability. Requirements: (1) per-team chargeback by GCP labels; (2) anomaly detection on daily spend; (3) forecast budget alerts at 50/80/100% with Pub/Sub for automation; (4) ad-hoc SQL slicing of detailed cost line items; (5) recommendations for rightsizing. Which combination BEST fits?
options: Cloud Billing reports in the console only, with daily review by the FinOps team and weekly slide updates to engineering leads
Billing budgets with Pub/Sub; BigQuery billing export for SQL/label chargeback; Monitoring anomaly detection on spend; Active Assist Recommender
Manual spreadsheet maintained weekly by the FinOps team using exports from the Cloud Billing reports UI plus per-team labels for chargeback
Cloud Asset Inventory exports to BigQuery only, with per-team SQL views and a Cloud Function emitting alerts on resource-count growth above a baseline
explanation: Each requirement maps to a feature: budgets+Pub/Sub for alerts/automation, BigQuery export for chargeback/SQL, Monitoring anomaly detection on a derived metric, and Recommender for rightsizing. Reports/spreadsheets/CAI alone miss most requirements.

text: Which Google SRE concept defines a measurable property of a service that you actually care about (e.g., request latency, availability)?
options: Service-Level Indicator (SLI)
Service-Level Agreement (SLA)
Toil index
Error budget
explanation: SLIs are the measured properties (e.g., 99.9% of requests under 200 ms). SLAs are external contracts based on SLOs, error budgets are the allowable failure budget derived from SLOs, and 'toil index' is not a standard SRE term.

doc_links.title: Site Reliability Engineering — SLIs, SLOs, SLAs

text: Which SRE practice replaces ad-hoc heroics with systematic improvements following an incident?
options: Blame-the-on-call review
Blameless postmortems with action items
Silent rollbacks
Disabling alerts after a noisy night
explanation: Blameless postmortems focus on systemic causes and durable action items, not individual blame. The other options either harm trust or hide problems.

text: An SRE team finds dev pushes are constantly breaking the SLO. Which SRE-aligned response is BEST?
options: Block all dev pushes to the affected services indefinitely until the team has rewritten the change-management process from scratch
Use the depleted error budget as a signal to halt feature releases until reliability work restores it
Increase the published SLO target so the budget is no longer depleted and the team can keep shipping features at the current cadence
Move the on-call rotation to dev only so the engineers shipping the breaking changes feel the pain of the resulting pages directly
explanation: Error-budget policy uses budget exhaustion as a structured signal to pause risky feature work and invest in reliability — the canonical SRE practice. Indefinite blocks, weakening the SLO to fit reality, or punitive rotations are all anti-patterns.

text: An SRE team is asked to take ownership of a service that has no SLOs, no runbooks, and frequent pages. Which is the FIRST step Google SRE recommends?
options: Refuse to onboard the service until the development team has independently authored runbooks and resolved every existing alert
Define SLIs/SLOs based on user-relevant signals, then iterate on alerting and reliability work
Immediately double the size of the on-call rotation and add a follow-the-sun handoff to absorb the existing page volume
Migrate the workload to GKE Autopilot first so Google manages the nodes and the team inherits fewer operational concerns
explanation: Establishing user-relevant SLIs/SLOs is the foundation that justifies alerting and reliability investment. Refusal is not a Google-SRE-aligned answer; staffing or platform changes don't address the missing measurement layer.

text: An organization has a 'You build it, you run it' DevOps culture but reliability is suffering at scale. Which SRE-aligned model BEST balances developer autonomy with reliability discipline?
options: Centralize all on-call rotations to a separate operations team and remove paging responsibility from the developer teams
Adopt an SRE partnership model with explicit engagement criteria, error budgets, and an exit clause that returns ops to dev when SLOs are missed
Eliminate on-call entirely and rely on best-effort response from whichever engineer is available during business hours
Have developers continue to operate solo without SRE involvement and add reliability work to the existing product backlog
explanation: The Google SRE engagement model is a partnership with explicit criteria, error budgets, and an exit clause back to dev teams when reliability standards are missed. Centralizing all ops or eliminating on-call breaks the shared-responsibility dynamic SRE depends on.

text: An SRE leader wants to measure DevOps performance against industry benchmarks (deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, MTTR). Which framework is the canonical reference?
options: ITIL v4
DORA Four Keys metrics
COBIT
explanation: DORA's Four Keys are the industry-standard DevOps performance metrics. ITIL/COBIT are broader IT governance frameworks; ISO 27001 is information security.

text: An SLO of 99.9% availability over 30 days corresponds to roughly how much allowed unavailability (the error budget)?
options: About 8.6 seconds
About 43.2 minutes
About 7.2 hours
About 30 days (no budget)
explanation: 30 days × 0.1% = ~43.2 minutes of allowed unavailability. The other figures correspond to 99.9999%, 99% and a 100%-target SLO.

text: What does 'SLI' stand for in SRE?
options: Service-Level Index
Service-Level Indicator
Site Latency Indicator
Standard Logging Interface
explanation: An SLI is a Service-Level Indicator — a measured ratio (e.g., good events / total events) that backs an SLO. The other expansions are not standard SRE terms.

text: Which SLI is MOST appropriate for a request-driven HTTP API?
options: CPU utilization on backends
The ratio of HTTP requests served successfully under a latency threshold (e.g., 99th percentile < 300 ms) to total requests
Number of pods running
Memory headroom
explanation: User-relevant SLIs measure end-user experience (success ratio, latency thresholds), not internal system health like CPU/memory or pod count. Those are useful for diagnosis but not as SLIs.

text: An SLO target of 99.95% leaves about 21.6 minutes of monthly error budget. The team currently has 18 minutes of failure logged this month and 10 days remaining. Which response is MOST aligned with error-budget policy?
options: Halt feature releases and shift focus to reliability work for the rest of the month
Lower the SLO target to 99.5%
Release the largest backlog of features in the next two days
Stop alerting until next month
explanation: When budget is nearly exhausted, the canonical response is to slow risky changes and invest in reliability. Lowering the SLO to fit is anti-pattern, batching risky releases burns the remaining budget, and silencing alerts hides the problem.

text: An SRE team uses Cloud Monitoring SLOs and wants alerting that fires when the error budget is being burned at a rate that would exhaust it before period-end. Which strategy is BEST?
options: Static threshold of 1% error rate
Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting (e.g., short window 14.4x and long window 6x burn rates)
One alert for any non-200 HTTP response
Page only when SLO is already breached
explanation: Google SRE recommends multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerts to balance speed of detection against precision — firing when burn rate threatens to exhaust budget within the SLO window. Static thresholds, per-error pages, and 'fire after breach' all miss the budget-burn semantic.

text: A user-facing service depends on five backend services with their own SLOs. The product team wants a realistic end-to-end availability SLO. Which approach is MOST defensible?
options: Set the SLO to the highest individual backend SLO
Multiply the dependent SLOs (worst case) and account for non-critical dependencies and graceful degradation when computing the achievable SLO
Pick 99.999% and pressure backends to match
Don't define an SLO until every backend exceeds 99.99%
explanation: End-to-end availability is bounded by the product of dependencies you actually need on the critical path; dependencies you can fall back on or queue around can be excluded. Picking the highest backend, demanding five-nines without analysis, or refusing to define an SLO are all unrealistic.

text: What is the primary purpose of a blameless postmortem?
options: Identify which individual engineer is responsible for the incident so HR can apply appropriate disciplinary action
Find root contributing factors and produce durable systemic action items
Justify the size and structure of the on-call schedule by documenting how often each rotation gets paged
Reduce the volume of alerting by silencing noisy monitors that triggered during the incident response window
explanation: Blameless postmortems focus on systemic causes and concrete action items so the same incident doesn't recur. Disciplining individuals erodes trust and discourages reporting; scheduling and alert volume are separate concerns.

text: During an incident, which role is responsible for coordinating the response (delegating tasks, communicating updates) according to Google's incident management framework?
options: Operations Lead
Incident Commander (IC)
Communications Lead
Subject-Matter Expert
explanation: The Incident Commander coordinates the response and delegates to Ops, Comms, and SMEs. Each role exists, but coordination is the IC's job.

text: Mid-incident, the on-call sees that traffic is failing in one region. Which action is MOST aligned with Google's incident management practice?
options: Begin extensive root-cause investigation in production before mitigating
Mitigate first (e.g., shift traffic, roll back, drain region) and investigate root cause after stabilization
Page the entire engineering team immediately
Disable monitoring to reduce noise
explanation: Mitigation precedes investigation: stop user impact first, diagnose later. Investigating in production while users suffer, paging everyone, and disabling monitoring are all anti-patterns.

text: Which artifact captures who did what and when during an incident, supporting the postmortem timeline?
options: A central incident channel (chat) or a structured incident document maintained in real time
Personal engineer notebooks and local terminal scrollback compiled into a shared document several days after the incident
The on-call engineer's recollection written up from memory once the incident is resolved and they have rested
The customer-facing status-page summary and any external blog post published after the incident is fully closed
explanation: A real-time, shared incident channel or document is the standard timeline artifact, supporting the postmortem. Reconstructing from memory or personal notes loses fidelity.

text: An organization completes postmortems but the same kinds of incidents keep recurring. Which is the MOST likely systemic failure?
options: Postmortem documents are too short and lack a long enough narrative description of what happened during the incident timeline
Action items are not being prioritized, owned, and tracked to completion alongside feature work
Too many engineers attend the postmortem review meeting, diluting the discussion and making it hard to reach consensus
Postmortems are shared too widely across the company, causing teams to write less candid content out of reputational concern
explanation: If action items aren't tracked and prioritized like feature work, the lessons from postmortems don't translate into change — the most common systemic failure. Length, attendance, and visibility are not root causes for recurrence.

text: A team wants to reduce 'unknown unknowns' that lead to long incidents. Which proactive practice is MOST aligned with SRE?
options: Increase paging thresholds across all alert policies so that fewer incidents wake the on-call engineer outside of business hours
Run regular Disaster Recovery Testing (DiRT)/game days that exercise failure modes (zone outage, dependency failure, runbook gaps)
Adopt a strict change-freeze policy that avoids touching production except for security patches deemed urgent by leadership
Reduce monitoring coverage and alerting volume to lower observability spend and limit on-call cognitive load on the team
explanation: DiRT/game-days deliberately surface gaps in monitoring, runbooks, and architecture so they can be fixed before real incidents. Higher paging thresholds, freezing production, or cutting monitoring all increase blind spots.

text: Which definition matches Google SRE's concept of 'toil'?
options: Any work an engineer dislikes
Manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical, devoid of enduring value, and scaling linearly with service size
Any incident response work
All on-call work
explanation: Google SRE precisely defines toil as manual/repetitive/automatable work that scales with service size and lacks enduring value. Disliking work, incident response, and on-call are not the definition.

text: What target does Google SRE recommend as a maximum proportion of an SRE's time spent on toil?
options: About 50%
About 25%
About 80%
0% — all toil should be eliminated immediately
explanation: Google SRE caps toil at roughly 50% per SRE, with the remainder devoted to engineering work. 25%/80% are not the published guideline, and zero toil is unrealistic in practice.

text: Which automation reduces toil for the recurring task of provisioning a new project with the same network, IAM, and budget settings?
options: A wiki page describing manual steps
A 'project factory' implemented in Terraform / Infrastructure Manager invoked via a self-service request
A Slack channel where engineers ask owners to do it
Console clicks logged in audit logs
explanation: A Terraform-based project factory turns repetitive provisioning into a code-driven, self-service workflow — the canonical toil-reduction pattern. Wikis, ad-hoc requests, and console clicks are exactly the manual loops it eliminates.

text: Which Google Cloud service can automate routine response actions (e.g., restarting an unhealthy backend, scaling a queue) when an alert fires?
options: Cloud Functions / Cloud Run subscribed to Pub/Sub notification channels from Cloud Monitoring alerts
Cloud Source Repositories
Artifact Registry
Filestore
explanation: Wiring Monitoring alerts → Pub/Sub → Cloud Functions/Run gives event-driven automated remediation, reducing on-call toil. The other services don't perform automated runbook actions.

text: An SRE team measures toil at 65% per engineer. Which combination is MOST likely to durably reduce toil below 50% within two quarters?
options: Add SRE headcount to the rotation and continue the existing manual operational processes that generated the toil
Identify the top 3 toil drivers, fund engineering work to automate them, track toil-hours weekly, and freeze new toil-creating workflows
Stop measuring toil percentage to remove the perceived pressure on engineers and let the rotation self-balance
Outsource on-call to a managed operations vendor and hand off the runbooks for routine incident response work
explanation: Targeted automation of the top toil drivers, with measurement and a freeze on new toil sources, is the engineering-first SRE approach. Adding people without removing toil scales the same problem; abandoning measurement or outsourcing transfers rather than solves it.

text: A team wants to safely automate destructive remediation actions (terminating instances, draining traffic) in response to alerts. Which design is BEST?
options: Run remediation directly from the alert with full project owner permissions
Implement automation as a workflow with least-privilege service accounts, stage-by-stage circuit breakers, dry-run mode, audit logs, and a kill-switch
Manually click console buttons from a runbook
Email the on-call and wait
explanation: Destructive remediation needs least-privilege identity, circuit breakers, dry-run, auditability, and a kill-switch to stay safe. Owner-credentialed direct execution is high-blast-radius, manual clicks defeat automation, and email-and-wait reintroduces the toil.

text: Which Google Cloud service runs CI builds in containerized steps defined in YAML, with managed workers?
options: Cloud Build
Cloud Deploy
Cloud Source Repositories
Cloud Tasks
explanation: Cloud Build executes containerized build steps on managed workers. Cloud Deploy is for delivery, Source Repos hosts code, and Tasks is general queueing.

text: Which trigger type causes Cloud Build to run when a commit is pushed to a connected GitHub repo branch?
options: Manual trigger
Push (branch / tag) trigger
Pub/Sub trigger
Webhook trigger to an external service
explanation: Cloud Build push triggers fire on branch or tag pushes from a connected SCM. Manual triggers need explicit invocation, Pub/Sub triggers fire on messages, and webhook triggers handle external events.

text: A pipeline must run unit tests in parallel across 10 modules and only proceed to packaging when all pass. Which Cloud Build feature is the right fit?
options: Sequential build steps with `waitFor: ['-']`
Parallel build steps using `waitFor: ['-']` for the test steps and a packaging step that waits for all of them via `waitFor: [...]`
Multiple build triggers fired from a script
Cloud Tasks for each test
explanation: Cloud Build's `waitFor` directive lets steps run in parallel and a downstream step depend on the set, which is exactly the fan-out / fan-in pattern needed. Sequential steps are slower; multiple triggers don't coordinate; Cloud Tasks is not a build orchestrator.

text: A team's Cloud Build pipeline needs to deploy to a private GKE cluster that has no public endpoint. Which approach is REQUIRED?
options: Make the cluster public temporarily
Use Cloud Build private pools (or VPC-attached workers) so the build runs inside the VPC and reaches the private GKE control plane
Run the build on the engineer's laptop
Disable the cluster's authorized networks
explanation: Cloud Build private pools run workers in your VPC, providing private connectivity to private GKE control planes. Making the cluster public or disabling authorized networks defeats the security posture; running on a laptop bypasses CI controls.

text: A regulated team needs CI builds to run with no internet egress, fetching dependencies only from approved internal mirrors, and producing signed provenance. Which combination fits BEST?
options: Default Cloud Build pool with public internet access
Cloud Build private pools in a VPC with egress restricted via VPC-SC and a private Artifact Registry mirror, plus SLSA build provenance signing
Self-hosted Jenkins on a single laptop
Pull dependencies from npmjs.org over the public internet
explanation: Private pools in a perimeter-controlled VPC, mirrored Artifact Registry, and SLSA provenance together deliver an air-gapped, attestable supply chain. Default pools and public-internet fetches violate the egress and mirror requirements; self-hosted laptop CI fails every audit.

doc_links.title: Cloud Build private pools and VPC-SC

text: A monorepo of 60 services has 30-minute builds. Which Cloud Build optimization is MOST impactful?
options: Run every build on the largest possible machine type
Use change-detection to build only affected services + remote build cache (e.g., kaniko cache, Bazel remote cache via Cloud Storage)
Disable tests
Build everything on every commit on the default machine
explanation: Change-aware builds plus remote caching collapse build time by avoiding repeated work — the canonical monorepo CI optimization. Larger machines help marginally but don't avoid redundant work; disabling tests is unsafe; building everything every time is the baseline cost being optimized.

text: Which Artifact Registry feature scans container images for known CVEs?
options: Vulnerability scanning (Artifact Analysis)
Tag immutability
Repository labels
Cross-region replication
explanation: Artifact Analysis scans Artifact Registry images for OS and language-package vulnerabilities. The other features are about lifecycle/metadata, not scanning.

text: Which feature prevents an image tag (e.g., `v1.2.3`) from being overwritten in Artifact Registry?
options: Tag immutability
IAM
VPC-SC
Cloud Build approval
explanation: Tag immutability prevents reuse of the same tag for a different digest, ensuring deployments are reproducible. IAM controls who can push, VPC-SC controls perimeter, and Cloud Build approval gates builds.

text: Which Google Cloud capability ensures only signed, approved container images can be deployed to GKE clusters?
options: Pod Security Admission only
Binary Authorization with attestation policies
Network policies
Cluster autoscaler
explanation: Binary Authorization enforces deploy-time policy that admits only images carrying valid attestations. PSA controls pod security primitives; network policies handle traffic; autoscaler manages capacity.

text: A team wants curated, vetted open-source Java packages with security metadata served from Google instead of pulling from Maven Central directly. Which option fits BEST?
options: Assured Open Source Software (Assured OSS) packages from Google
Public Maven Central with a custom mirror script
Dependabot only
Cloud Build local cache
explanation: Assured OSS provides Google-curated, scanned, and signed builds of common open-source packages, including Java. A custom mirror, Dependabot, or local cache don't provide Google-vetted artifacts with attestations.

text: An organization needs end-to-end software supply-chain security across CI, build, registry, and deploy on Google Cloud. Which integrated offering is the canonical fit?
options: Cloud Build alone, with manual reviewer sign-off on each Pull Request before merge to the release branch
Software Delivery Shield (Cloud Build SLSA provenance, Artifact Registry vuln scanning, Assured OSS, Binary Authorization)
Cloud Logging alone, with log-based metrics that fire on suspicious build steps or unexpected image pushes
Cloud Armor with custom rules in front of the deployed services to block exploit attempts at the edge
explanation: Software Delivery Shield is Google's integrated supply-chain security solution covering build provenance, artifact scanning, OSS curation, and deploy-time enforcement. The other choices each address only one slice.

text: A platform team wants to enforce that production deployments may use ONLY images built within the last 30 days, signed by the production CI service account, and free of CVEs scoring ≥ 7. Which approach satisfies all three?
options: Manual code review of every Dockerfile and CI config before each release branch is cut and tagged for production
Binary Authorization policy combining attestor checks (CI-signed), freshness attestations, and Artifact Analysis vuln-scan attestations
Cloud Logging alerts on unscanned images that page the on-call rotation when a deploy attempts to use them
Disable Artifact Registry public access and require all image pulls to traverse Private Service Connect endpoints
explanation: Binary Authorization can compose multiple attestor requirements (build identity, freshness, vuln-scan) so all three constraints are evaluated at admission. Manual review and log alerts don't enforce; disabling the registry breaks deployments.

text: Which deployment strategy replaces all old instances with new ones at once, causing brief downtime?
options: Rolling update
Recreate (big-bang)
Canary
Blue/Green
explanation: Recreate stops the old version then starts the new, incurring downtime. Rolling, canary, and blue/green minimize or avoid downtime.

text: Which Cloud Run feature enables blue/green deployments by deploying a revision with no traffic, then switching 100% to it?
options: `--no-traffic` deployment flag combined with traffic switching to the new revision
Concurrency setting
Min instances
VPC connector
explanation: Deploying a Cloud Run revision with `--no-traffic` then switching traffic to it implements blue/green. Concurrency, min-instances, and VPC connector are unrelated controls.

text: A team uses Cloud Deploy to roll out a GKE service. They want a canary that goes 10% → 50% → 100% with verification between phases. Which Cloud Deploy feature provides this?
options: Cloud Deploy standard rollouts that replace all pods at once and rely on a separate post-deploy smoke test for verification
Canary deployment strategy with phases and (optionally) verify steps
Chained Cloud Build triggers where each build step deploys to a larger subset of pods using kubectl scale commands
Manual `kubectl apply` of progressively larger Deployment manifests with engineers eyeballing dashboards between steps
explanation: Cloud Deploy's canary strategy supports user-defined phases and verification jobs between them. Standard rollouts replace at once; chained Cloud Build triggers and manual `kubectl` lack the managed canary semantics.

text: Which deployment strategy is BEST when database schema changes require expand-then-contract migrations to remain compatible with both versions?
options: Recreate
Rolling update / canary with backward- and forward-compatible schema (expand-then-contract over multiple releases)
Big-bang during business hours
Skip migrations and edit data manually
explanation: Expand-then-contract requires the application to support both schemas during the transition, which a rolling/canary release naturally provides. Big-bang and recreate force everyone onto one version; manual edits are unsafe and unauditable.

text: A team wants to canary at the network layer for a Cloud Run service so they can route by header (e.g., `x-canary: true`) and by percentage. Which design fits BEST?
options: Use Cloud Run native traffic splitting only, which routes a fixed percentage of requests to each revision tag
Front Cloud Run with an external Application Load Balancer using serverless NEGs and URL-map rules that match headers and weighted backends
Use Cloud DNS round-robin between two Cloud Run service URLs to distribute traffic to the new revision
Cloud Run revisions cannot be canaried by header and require running the canary as a separate Cloud Run service
explanation: An external HTTPS LB with serverless NEGs supports header-based and weighted routing across Cloud Run revisions — covering both header-targeted and percentage canaries. Native Cloud Run splitting is percentage-only, DNS round-robin is coarse and cached, and Cloud Run does support canaries.

text: A team needs progressive delivery with automated rollback driven by SLO/error-rate metrics during the canary phase. Which Cloud Deploy feature should they use?
options: Manual approvals at every step
Custom canary `verify` jobs that consult Cloud Monitoring SLO/metrics and fail the rollout to trigger automated rollback
Cloud Build triggers
Static delays between phases
explanation: Cloud Deploy supports custom verification jobs in canary phases that can query Cloud Monitoring; failing verification rolls back automatically. Manual approvals don't react to metrics, Cloud Build alone lacks the rollout context, and static delays don't observe.

text: Which technique injects a small amount of synthetic traffic into production to detect regressions early?
options: Synthetic monitoring / synthetic traffic
Recreate deploys
Disabling logging
Static code analysis
explanation: Synthetic monitoring runs scripted requests in production to validate user-relevant flows continuously. Recreate deploys, disabling logging, and SCA don't exercise prod behavior live.

text: Which deployment technique mirrors real production traffic to a new version without serving its responses to users?
options: Shadow / mirror traffic
Big-bang deploy
Recreate
Static A/B test
explanation: Shadow traffic copies real requests to a new version for validation while users still receive the original responses. Big-bang/recreate are full cutovers, and static A/B testing typically directs real users to variants.

text: A team wants to run a chaos-engineering experiment against a production GKE service to verify resilience. Which approach is the SAFEST starting point?
options: Drop network for the entire region during peak hours unannounced
Run a scoped experiment (e.g., kill 1% of pods or inject latency on one zone) within an error-budget allowance, with a fast abort and clear rollback
Disable monitoring during the experiment
Test only in dev
explanation: Chaos experiments should be small, scoped, observable, and reversible, ideally within budget. Region-wide blackouts are reckless, disabling monitoring removes the signal, and dev-only testing doesn't validate production characteristics.

text: Which technique lets a team release a code path to all instances but enable it for a small subset of users via runtime configuration?
options: Feature flags / dynamic configuration
Recreate deploys
Static container images
Deleting alternate code paths at compile time
explanation: Feature flags decouple deploy from release, enabling progressive exposure and quick rollback without redeploying. Recreate deploys, static images, and compile-time pruning all couple release to deploy.

text: A team wants to safely test ML inference revisions in production for parity. Which combination of techniques fits BEST?
options: Hard cutover at midnight
Shadow traffic to the new revision + offline output diffing + small canary gated by SLO verification before full rollout
Deploy to dev only and ship from there
Disable logging during validation
explanation: Shadow traffic + diffing + gated canary is the canonical safe pattern for ML inference parity. Hard cutover ignores parity risk, dev-only doesn't validate production behavior, and disabled logging removes the validation signal.

text: An organization wants 'testing in production' to be culturally safe. Which set of practices BEST reduces blast radius?
options: Allowing only senior engineers to push to prod
