Feature flags + progressive delivery + automated rollback + SLO-based gates + blameless postmortems on incidents that result
Avoid testing in prod entirely
Encourage manual hot-fixing
explanation: The DORA-aligned answer combines flags, progressive delivery, automated rollback, SLO gates, and blameless culture so that experimentation is bounded and learning. Restricting pushes by seniority, prohibiting prod testing, or hot-fixing manually all undermine reliability.

text: Which Google Cloud service provides managed Terraform execution with state, drift detection, and IAM-aware deployments?
options: Infrastructure Manager
Cloud Build
Cloud Functions
Deployment Manager (recommended for new projects)
explanation: Infrastructure Manager is Google's managed Terraform service with state and IAM-aware deployments. Cloud Build/Functions are general compute, and Deployment Manager is in maintenance — not recommended for new projects.

text: Which file in a Terraform module declares input parameters?
options: `outputs.tf`
`variables.tf`
`providers.tf`
`terraform.tfstate`
explanation: `variables.tf` (by convention) declares input variables. `outputs.tf` declares outputs, `providers.tf` configures providers, and `terraform.tfstate` is the state file.

text: Where should Terraform state for shared infrastructure be stored?
options: On each engineer's laptop in the default local backend, committed alongside the .tf files into the application's Git repository
A versioned, encrypted Cloud Storage bucket with state locking via the GCS backend
An unversioned Cloud Storage bucket with allUsers read access so any team member can fetch the latest state without auth
In a Cloud SQL table updated by a custom CI step that serializes the JSON state on each successful terraform apply run
explanation: Remote state in a versioned, encrypted GCS bucket with locking is the recommended Terraform pattern on GCP. Local state breaks team collaboration; unversioned/public state is a security and recovery risk.

text: Which IaC pattern keeps environment-specific configuration out of reusable modules?
options: Hard-code prod values inside the module
Use input variables and per-environment root configurations (e.g., dev/, staging/, prod/) that pass values to the same module
Maintain separate forks per environment
Edit state files directly
explanation: Per-environment root configs feeding the same module via variables is the canonical reuse pattern. Hard-coding, forking, and editing state are all maintenance hazards.

doc_links.title: Terraform best practices on Google Cloud

text: A platform team wants every Terraform plan reviewed in a PR with policy-as-code checks (e.g., 'no public buckets', 'mandatory labels') before apply. Which combination fits BEST?
options: Run terraform apply locally from each engineer's laptop and ask reviewers to inspect the captured plan logs in chat
PR-driven plans via Cloud Build / Infrastructure Manager with policy-as-code (OPA / Conftest, Organization Policy, Policy Controller) gating the apply
Skip review for any change a senior engineer labels low-risk and apply it directly to the main branch
Disable provider authentication on the Terraform service account so unsafe plans cannot reach Google Cloud APIs
explanation: PR-driven plans + policy-as-code gates the apply on machine-checkable rules, not human-only review. Local apply, skipping review, and disabling auth all undermine the control model.

text: An org has 200 projects managed by Terraform. They want to detect when a resource has been modified outside of Terraform (drift). Which approach fits BEST?
options: Have each project owner perform a quarterly manual review of the console and compare it to the team's Terraform repository
Run scheduled `terraform plan` (via Cloud Build or Infrastructure Manager) to diff state against reality, and use Cloud Asset Inventory feeds for real-time change detection
Disable Terraform writes whenever drift is detected and switch the project to console-only management until drift is resolved
Recreate each Google Cloud project monthly from a fresh Terraform apply so any out-of-band changes are erased automatically
explanation: Scheduled `plan` runs surface drift, and Asset Inventory feeds catch out-of-band changes in real time — standard drift-detection on GCP. Manual checks don't scale, disabling Terraform compounds drift, and monthly recreation is destructive.

text: Which Google Cloud service provides centralized application configuration with versioning and runtime delivery to apps?
options: Cloud Storage objects only
Secret Manager (for secrets) and — for non-secret config — a versioned Cloud Storage bucket or App Config patterns via Firestore/Cloud Storage
Memorystore
Cloud Tasks
explanation: Secret Manager is for sensitive values; non-secret runtime config is typically delivered via versioned GCS objects or Firestore documents read at startup or via watch. Memorystore and Cloud Tasks are not config stores.

text: Which Kubernetes object stores non-secret application configuration data as key/value pairs?
options: Secret
ConfigMap
ServiceAccount
PodDisruptionBudget
explanation: ConfigMaps store non-secret config; Secrets store sensitive data (and should use Secret Manager + CSI driver where possible). ServiceAccount and PDB serve different roles.

text: A GKE workload needs DB credentials kept in Secret Manager and mounted as a file in the pod, refreshing automatically when the secret rotates. Which approach is RECOMMENDED?
options: Bake the credential into the image
Use the Secret Manager CSI driver (Secret Store CSI) to mount Secret Manager secrets as files
Hard-code in a ConfigMap
Pass via container environment variable from `kubectl edit`
explanation: Secret Store CSI with the Google provider mounts Secret Manager secrets directly into pods and supports rotation. Baking into images, ConfigMaps, or manual envs all leak or stagnate.

text: Which approach BEST avoids drift between application configuration in code and what's running in production?
options: Edit configuration in the console at runtime
Treat configuration as code, store in Git, deliver via CI/CD or GitOps (Config Sync) with the cluster reconciling to the repo
Email config changes to operators
Use untracked Helm `values.yaml` per cluster
explanation: Configuration-as-code under Git with Config Sync (or CI/CD) reconciliation is the canonical drift-elimination pattern. Console edits, email, and untracked overrides are the drift sources.

text: A multi-region service needs feature-flag changes to take effect within seconds, with a centralized audit and the ability to roll back instantly. Which architecture fits BEST?
options: Restart every service replica after each flag change so the new value is read once at process startup time
Use a managed feature-flag/config service with low-latency client SDKs (or Firestore real-time listeners), with PR-driven changes for audit
Email engineers to flip the flag at the next deploy window and bundle the change with the next planned release
Hard-code per-region YAML config files in each service's repository and redeploy when a flag needs to change
explanation: A push/subscribe config or feature-flag service (or Firestore real-time listeners) gives sub-second propagation, and PR-driven changes give audit. Restart-required flags fail the latency requirement; email/YAML can't react in seconds.

text: Which combination provides safe environment-specific configuration delivery to GKE workloads at scale?
options: A single ConfigMap repository covering all environments, applied manually with kubectl by the platform on-call engineer
Per-env ConfigMaps/Secrets generated by Kustomize or Helm, delivered via Config Sync from per-env Git folders, with Policy Controller enforcing keys
Manual kubectl edit of each ConfigMap in each cluster whenever a configuration change needs to be rolled out
Hard-code environment-specific configuration values in per-environment Dockerfiles and rebuild images per environment
explanation: Kustomize/Helm + Config Sync from per-env Git paths + Policy Controller is the canonical scaled config delivery on GKE. Manual application, `kubectl edit`, and Dockerfile hard-coding don't scale or stay consistent.

text: Which Google Cloud service stores secrets with versioning, IAM-controlled access, and audit logging?
options: Cloud KMS
Secret Manager
Cloud Storage with public ACL
Cloud Logging
explanation: Secret Manager is the dedicated secrets store with versioning, IAM, and audit logging. Cloud KMS manages keys (often used to protect Secret Manager itself), GCS isn't a secrets service, and Logging is for logs.

text: Which IAM principle should govern access to a production database password in Secret Manager?
options: Project Owner for everyone
Least privilege: only the workload's service account gets `roles/secretmanager.secretAccessor` on that specific secret
Public read
Domain-wide delegation
explanation: Least privilege — the specific workload SA gets accessor on the specific secret — is the canonical pattern. Owner everywhere, public read, and domain-wide delegation all violate least privilege.

text: A team wants to rotate a database password every 30 days and have applications pick up the new password without redeploying. Which approach is BEST?
options: Edit the password as a constant in source code and roll out a fresh container image to all replicas every 30 days
Store the password in Secret Manager with a rotation schedule (Pub/Sub notifications), and have the app read the latest version or use the Secret Manager CSI driver
Email the new password to engineers monthly and ask them to update each environment's configuration during a maintenance window
Disable the database during the rotation window and re-enable it after every application has been redeployed with the new credentials
explanation: Secret Manager rotation (Pub/Sub + version) plus on-demand read or CSI sync is the canonical zero-downtime rotation pattern. Source code edits, email, or disabling the DB are not viable.

text: A CI pipeline running in Cloud Build needs short-lived credentials to a third-party SaaS API instead of long-lived static keys. Which approach is RECOMMENDED?
options: Hard-code static API keys in the build YAML
Use Workload Identity Federation from Google Cloud to the third-party (or vice versa) so the build exchanges its Google identity for short-lived SaaS credentials
Store the static key in a public bucket
Pass keys as plain environment variables in the trigger config
explanation: Workload Identity Federation enables keyless, short-lived credentials between providers, removing static-key risk. The other options leak or persist credentials.

text: An org needs CMEK-protected secrets, organization-level access reviews, and the ability to revoke a secret across all consumers immediately. Which approach fits BEST?
options: Store secrets in a Cloud Storage bucket protected by default Google-managed encryption keys with bucket-level IAM grants per consumer
Secret Manager with CMEK keys in Cloud KMS, group-based IAM, VPC-SC perimeter, and version disable/destroy for instant revocation
Email secrets to the consuming application owners and ask them to inject the values into their workloads via private deployment pipelines
Drop a per-VM secrets file into a managed startup script and rotate it via crontab on each VM that consumes the secret
explanation: Secret Manager + CMEK + group-based IAM + VPC-SC + version disable/destroy gives encryption control, auditable access, perimeter, and instant revocation. The other options fail one or more of those requirements.

text: A GKE workload should authenticate to Google Cloud APIs with no service-account JSON keys. Which design is correct?
options: Mount a downloaded SA key JSON as a Secret
Enable GKE Workload Identity, bind the Kubernetes ServiceAccount to a Google ServiceAccount, and let the workload obtain short-lived tokens via the metadata server
Use the node's default service account with Editor role
Hard-code an OAuth refresh token
explanation: GKE Workload Identity is the keyless way to bind KSAs to GSAs and provide short-lived tokens. Mounted SA keys, broad node SAs, and hard-coded refresh tokens are all anti-patterns.

text: Which GKE mode has Google manage the nodes and node pools, billing per-pod resources, with strong defaults?
options: GKE Standard
GKE Autopilot
GKE on-prem only
Cloud Run
explanation: GKE Autopilot fully manages nodes and bills per pod. Standard requires you to size and operate node pools; Cloud Run is a different platform.

text: Which Kubernetes feature ensures a minimum number of replica pods stay available during voluntary disruptions like node upgrades?
options: PodDisruptionBudget
HorizontalPodAutoscaler
ResourceQuota
NetworkPolicy
explanation: PDB protects availability during voluntary disruptions. HPA scales replicas, ResourceQuota limits namespace usage, NetworkPolicy controls traffic.

text: A team wants GKE node OS, kernel, and container runtime patched automatically with minimum disruption. Which configuration is BEST?
options: Disable auto-upgrade and patch manually monthly
Use release channels (Regular/Stable) with auto-upgrade enabled, maintenance windows, and PDBs to control disruption
Run nodes on a fixed image forever
Switch all nodes to a single zone to simplify upgrades
explanation: Release channels with auto-upgrade plus maintenance windows and PDBs balance currency and stability. Manual patching ages clusters, fixed images accumulate vulnerabilities, single-zone increases blast radius.

text: Which pair of GKE features allows pods to scale on CPU/custom metrics AND adds nodes when needed?
options: HPA + Cluster Autoscaler (Standard) or built-in node management (Autopilot)
VPA only
PDB + NetworkPolicy
Manual node count edits
explanation: HPA scales pods; Cluster Autoscaler (or Autopilot's built-in management) adds/removes nodes to fit pending pods. VPA resizes pods, PDB/NP are unrelated to scaling, and manual edits aren't autoscaling.

text: A regulated workload needs hardware-isolated tenants on GKE. Which feature is the canonical fit?
options: Apply Pod Security Standards in restricted mode across the GKE namespaces and rely on the standard GKE node pools
Sole-tenant nodes (GCE) backing dedicated GKE Standard node pools, plus GKE Sandbox (gVisor) for additional in-node isolation
Configure Calico NetworkPolicy across the cluster to deny cross-namespace traffic and treat that as the tenant isolation boundary
Enable Workload Identity for the regulated workload's service accounts so each tenant can authenticate to Google APIs separately
explanation: Sole-tenant nodes give physical-host isolation, and GKE Sandbox adds in-node syscall isolation — the canonical hardware/runtime isolation answer. PSS, NetworkPolicy, and WI address other concerns.

text: An SRE team wants to operate 50 GKE clusters as a fleet, with consistent policy, multi-cluster service discovery, and centralized config. Which Google offering is the canonical fit?
options: Manage each cluster independently with separate `kubectl` contexts
GKE Enterprise (Anthos) with Fleet management, Config Sync, Policy Controller, and Multi-cluster Services / Multi-cluster Ingress
Cloud Run only
Switch to Cloud Functions
explanation: GKE Enterprise's fleet model with Config Sync, Policy Controller, and MCS/MCI is the canonical multi-cluster operating model. Independent management doesn't scale, and Run/Functions aren't Kubernetes fleets.

text: Which Cloud Logging concept routes log entries to a destination such as BigQuery, Cloud Storage, or Pub/Sub?
options: Log sink
Metric descriptor
Notification channel
Snapshot
explanation: Log sinks route entries to supported destinations. Metric descriptors describe metrics, notification channels are for alerts, and snapshots are unrelated.

text: Which log bucket holds logs by default in every Google Cloud project?
options: `_Required` and `_Default`
`_System`
`_App`
`_GCE`
explanation: Cloud Logging provisions `_Required` (admin/audit) and `_Default` (other) buckets per project. The other names are not standard Cloud Logging buckets.

text: A team needs to query logs across many projects with SQL and join with business tables. Which approach is BEST?
options: Per-project log explorer queries combined manually
Aggregated organization-level sink to a BigQuery dataset (or Log Analytics-enabled bucket) with partitioned tables
Export logs to local disk for grep
Disable logging to save cost
explanation: Aggregated org sinks to BigQuery (or Log Analytics) give SQL across many projects with retention. Per-project manual queries don't scale, local grep loses durability, and disabling logging breaks compliance.

text: Which feature lets you generate a numeric metric from log entries (e.g., counter of 5xx) for use in alerts?
options: Log-based metrics
Logs Explorer histograms only
Cloud Trace spans
Cloud Profiler reports
explanation: Log-based metrics convert log matches into time-series usable in Monitoring alerts. Histograms visualize but aren't alertable, traces and profiles are different telemetry types.

text: An org needs immutable retention of admin/audit logs for 7 years across all projects. Which design fits BEST?
options: Default `_Required` retention only (typically 400 days)
Aggregated org-level sink for Admin Activity logs to a Cloud Storage bucket with Bucket Lock retention of 7 years (and/or BigQuery dataset retention)
Per-project manual exports to a laptop
Disable Audit Logs to reduce volume
explanation: Org-aggregated sinks + Bucket Lock satisfy multi-project, immutable, 7-year retention. Default retention is too short; manual exports aren't durable; disabling audit logs is non-compliant.

text: A team's logs include sensitive customer data that must be redacted before storage. Which design is RECOMMENDED?
options: Rely on developers to remember not to log sensitive fields
Use Cloud DLP / Sensitive Data Protection inspection + de-identification in the logging pipeline (e.g., via Pub/Sub → Dataflow → sink) before persisting to long-term storage
Encrypt the bucket and consider it solved
Lower log level to ERROR only
explanation: Sensitive Data Protection-driven de-identification before persistence is the canonical answer for log redaction at scale. Hopeful developer practice, encryption alone, and lowering log level don't redact sensitive content.

text: Which Cloud Monitoring object visualizes time-series with charts arranged on a single page?
options: Dashboard
Alert policy
Notification channel
Service-level objective
explanation: Dashboards arrange time-series charts. Alert policies fire when conditions are met, channels deliver notifications, SLOs measure reliability targets.

text: Which query language does Cloud Monitoring support natively for advanced metric queries (in addition to PromQL)?
options: MQL (Monitoring Query Language)
T-SQL
GraphQL
Cypher
explanation: Cloud Monitoring supports MQL and PromQL. T-SQL/GraphQL/Cypher are unrelated to monitoring queries.

text: An SRE team needs a PromQL-compatible managed metric backend for their open-source Grafana dashboards on GCP. Which service fits BEST?
options: Cloud Logging
Managed Service for Prometheus (and Cloud Monitoring's PromQL endpoint)
Bigtable
Cloud Trace
explanation: Managed Service for Prometheus is Google's managed Prometheus offering that integrates with Grafana via PromQL. Logging holds logs, Bigtable is a NoSQL DB, Trace is for spans.

text: Which type of dashboard chart BEST represents the distribution of request latency over time?
options: Stacked area of error counts
Heatmap of histogram-distribution metrics (e.g., p50/p90/p99 lines, or distribution heatmap)
Pie chart of HTTP codes
Single scalar of mean latency
explanation: Heatmaps/percentile lines reveal the distribution and tails of latency. Means hide tails, pie charts don't show time, and error counts answer a different question.

text: An organization wants standardized 'golden signals' dashboards (latency, traffic, errors, saturation) auto-provisioned for every microservice. Which approach is BEST?
options: Have each microservice team build and maintain its own dashboards manually in the Cloud Monitoring console for each environment
Define dashboards as code (Cloud Monitoring dashboard JSON or Terraform google_monitoring_dashboard) and deploy them via CI alongside service infra
Take screenshots of one team's golden-signal dashboards and store them in a wiki for other teams to reproduce by hand in their projects
Use only the default Cloud Monitoring dashboards that ship with each Google Cloud service and accept that they are not service-specific
explanation: Dashboard-as-code (Terraform `google_monitoring_dashboard` or JSON) lets golden-signal templates be auto-provisioned per service consistently. Manual or screenshot-based approaches don't scale; defaults aren't service-specific.

text: An SRE team is alert-fatigued. They want to keep dashboards rich while drastically reducing pages. Which strategy is MOST aligned with SRE practice?
options: Disable alerts overnight so on-call engineers can sleep through low-priority pages and triage them in the morning
Replace symptom-noisy threshold alerts with a small set of SLO burn-rate alerts (multi-window, multi-burn-rate); keep dashboards for diagnosis
Page on every production error so the on-call rotation builds an intuition for which error categories are worth deeper investigation
Send all Cloud Monitoring alerts to a shared team mailbox and let the on-call sweep the inbox once per shift
explanation: SLO burn-rate alerting drives down false-positive pages by tying alerts to user-impacting budget burn. The other options either hide problems or worsen fatigue.

text: Which Google Cloud service collects distributed traces of requests as they flow through services?
options: Cloud Trace
Cloud Logging
Cloud Profiler
Cloud Monitoring
explanation: Cloud Trace stores and analyzes distributed traces. Logging is for logs, Profiler is for CPU/heap, Monitoring is for metrics.

text: Which open standard is recommended for instrumenting applications to emit traces (and metrics/logs) across vendors?
options: OpenTelemetry
SNMP v2
JMX
Syslog
explanation: OpenTelemetry is the CNCF standard for traces (and metrics/logs) and is the recommended instrumentation. SNMP/JMX/Syslog don't cover distributed tracing as a standard.

text: A team wants to find which downstream service contributes the most latency to a slow user request. Which observability data type is the BEST fit?
options: Aggregate Cloud Logging entries from each service and search them with a saved query to attribute slow requests to a downstream
Distributed traces with parent/child spans across services, viewed in Cloud Trace to see per-hop latency for a single request
Continuous profiles from Cloud Profiler showing CPU and heap usage across the fleet, sliced by service and deployment version
Static code analysis reports from each service's CI pipeline ranked by the number of suspected slow code paths per service
explanation: Distributed traces show per-service span durations on a single request, which is the right view for downstream-attribution. Logs lack spanning, profiles show in-process CPU/heap, SCA is unrelated.

text: Which Google Cloud service captures continuous CPU and heap profiles from production with low overhead?
options: Cloud Profiler
Cloud Trace
Cloud Logging
Cloud Build
explanation: Cloud Profiler does continuous, low-overhead profiling of CPU and heap. Trace handles spans, Logging handles logs, Cloud Build is for CI.

text: A multi-language microservice mesh wants vendor-neutral instrumentation that exports traces to Cloud Trace today and could switch backends later. Which architecture fits BEST?
options: Vendor-specific Cloud Trace SDK in every service
OpenTelemetry SDK in each service exporting via OTLP to an OpenTelemetry Collector configured to send to Cloud Trace (and optionally other backends)
Custom logging-only spans
JMX exporter only
explanation: OTel SDKs + Collector lets you swap exporters without changing app code, and Cloud Trace is a supported destination. Vendor-specific SDKs lock you in; custom logs and JMX don't model distributed spans.

text: A high-traffic service can't trace 100% of requests but must keep a representative sample of slow / errored ones. Which sampling approach is BEST?
options: Head-based 1% random sampling at the service entry point, discarding all other traces before any span data leaves the process
Tail-based sampling at the OpenTelemetry Collector that retains all error/slow traces and a small percentage of normal ones
Enable tracing only at deployment time during the canary window and disable span emission once the rollout is complete
Disable tracing entirely and rely on Cloud Logging structured logs plus Cloud Monitoring metrics to investigate latency issues
explanation: Tail sampling at the Collector inspects whole traces and keeps the interesting ones (errors, high latency) plus a baseline. Head-based random sampling can drop the rare interesting cases; deploy-time and disabled tracing don't help.

text: Which Cloud Monitoring object delivers an alert (PagerDuty, email, Slack, Pub/Sub, etc.)?
options: Notification channel
Dashboard
Log bucket
Service
explanation: Notification channels deliver alerts to external destinations. Dashboards visualize, log buckets store logs, services are SLO targets.

text: Which page-routing tool is commonly integrated with Cloud Monitoring for on-call rotation and escalation?
options: PagerDuty (or similar like Opsgenie/Splunk On-Call)
Cloud Logging, the managed log aggregation service for storing, searching, and exporting application and platform logs
Artifact Registry, the managed repository for container images, language packages, and OS packages produced by your builds
Cloud Source Repositories, Google's hosted Git service used to store application source code with mirrored GitHub support
explanation: PagerDuty/Opsgenie are the standard on-call/escalation tools wired to Monitoring via webhooks/notification channels. The others are not paging tools.

text: Which design BEST reduces alert fatigue while still catching user-impacting issues?
options: Page on every error log line
Use SLO multi-window multi-burn-rate alerts as the primary paging signal; demote symptom-only alerts to ticket / dashboard severity
Send all alerts to email and turn off pager
Increase thresholds until no alerts fire
explanation: SLO burn-rate is the user-impact-aligned paging signal; symptom alerts move to non-paging severities. Per-line paging fatigues; killing the pager misses real issues; raising thresholds blindly hides them.

text: Which on-call practice protects engineers' health and reduces burnout?
options: On-call shifts of 7 days every week
Bounded shift length, follow-the-sun rotations, capped pages-per-shift, and time-off compensation for after-hours pages
No rotation — same engineer always
Page everyone for every alert
explanation: Bounded shifts, geographic rotations, page caps, and compensation are the standard humane on-call practices Google SRE recommends. Permanent on-call, single-engineer assignment, and broadcast pages all increase burnout.

text: An on-call engineer is paged 3 times for the same noisy alert in 30 minutes. Which Cloud Monitoring feature helps PRIMARILY?
options: Snooze + alert grouping/auto-close + tuning the policy condition (e.g., longer duration, ratio metrics, SLO burn-rate)
Disable Cloud Logging
Add more notification channels
Increase log retention
explanation: Snooze, grouping/auto-close, and tuning are the immediate and structural fixes for noisy alerts. Disabling logging or adding channels worsens the problem; retention is unrelated.

text: An organization wants policy-as-code for alerting (review, version, deploy alert policies via PR like infra). Which approach is BEST on GCP?
options: Edit alert policies directly in the Cloud Monitoring console and email a screenshot of the change diff to the team distribution list
Define alert policies as Terraform `google_monitoring_alert_policy` resources stored in Git, applied via CI/CD with PR review
Save before/after screenshots of every alert policy change to a shared drive folder for after-the-fact review by the team lead
Manage alert policies through a personal gcloud script run from each engineer's laptop whenever a thresholding change is needed
explanation: Terraform-managed alert policies under PR review give review, versioning, and reproducibility. Console edits, screenshots, and personal scripts break that control model.

text: Which Compute Engine feature automatically adds and removes VMs from a managed instance group based on load?
options: Managed instance group autoscaler
Sole-tenant nodes
Snapshots
Health-check ports
explanation: MIG autoscalers add/remove VMs based on metrics (CPU, custom). Sole-tenant nodes are about isolation; snapshots are backups; health-check ports are how LBs probe.

text: Which GKE component scales pods horizontally based on observed metrics?
options: HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA)
Cluster Autoscaler
PodDisruptionBudget
VerticalPodAutoscaler
explanation: HPA scales replica count by metrics. Cluster Autoscaler scales nodes; PDB protects availability; VPA resizes pod resource requests.

text: An SRE team needs capacity planning that survives a regional outage with no degradation. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Run a single region at full capacity and accept regional outages as planned downtime windows in the published service SLO
Run multi-region active-active with N+1 capacity per region (each region able to absorb the failed region's traffic)
Run an active primary region at full size plus a tiny standby region sized at 10% of peak, scaled up after the outage starts
Rely on autoscaling alone with no static headroom, trusting the autoscaler to absorb the failed region's traffic on demand
explanation: Multi-region active-active with N+1 per-region capacity ensures a failed region's load is absorbable. Single-region, tiny standby, or autoscale-only fail when a whole region drops.

text: A team needs to handle predictable traffic spikes (Black Friday) where autoscaling latency would cause SLO violations during the ramp. Which technique fits BEST?
options: Trust the existing autoscaler to react in real time to the Black Friday traffic ramp and accept brief SLO violations during the spike
Pre-warm capacity with scheduled autoscaler policies (or min-instances on Cloud Run / GKE) ahead of the predicted spike, plus a load-test rehearsal
Disable autoscaling during the Black Friday spike and run at a fixed maximum-size pool sized for the prior year's holiday peak
Lower the published service SLO during the spike window so the additional latency from cold starts does not count against the budget
explanation: Scheduled scaling and minimum-instances pre-warm capacity to avoid cold-start/lag during predictable spikes; load tests validate. Pure reactive scaling can lag; disabling scaling or weakening SLOs are not legitimate fixes.

text: A workload's queue depth grows during peak. The team wants pods to scale on queue length, not CPU. Which GKE feature enables this?
options: Configure HPA on CPU utilization only and tune the target percentage to scale earlier as the queue starts to grow during peak periods
HPA on custom/external metrics (e.g., Pub/Sub subscription backlog) via Cloud Monitoring or Managed Service for Prometheus
Enable Cluster Autoscaler with a low utilization target so additional nodes are added when the queue depth indirectly drives CPU usage
Use Vertical Pod Autoscaler in Auto mode to grow per-pod CPU/memory limits as queue processing time increases under heavier loads
explanation: HPA can scale on custom/external metrics like Pub/Sub backlog through the Monitoring/Prometheus adapters. CPU-only HPA misses queue dynamics; CA scales nodes; VPA resizes pods.

text: Which compute pricing option is appropriate for short, fault-tolerant batch jobs willing to be preempted?
options: Spot VMs
On-demand only
3-year CUDs
Sole-tenant nodes
explanation: Spot VMs are deeply discounted but can be preempted, ideal for fault-tolerant batch. On-demand pays full price; CUDs commit upfront; sole-tenant is about isolation.

text: Which Recommender output suggests downsizing or removing under-utilized VMs?
options: Idle VM and machine-type rightsizing recommendations
Container Analysis vulnerability findings, which scan pushed Artifact Registry images for known CVE entries
IAM Recommender's role-rightsizing output, which suggests revoking unused permissions from project principals
VPC Flow Logs network throughput report, summarizing per-flow bytes transferred between source and destination IPs
explanation: Active Assist's idle-VM and rightsizing recommendations target underused VMs. Vuln scans, IAM Recommender, and network reports address other domains.

text: A steady 24/7 GCE workload uses on-demand pricing. Which option will likely yield the LARGEST sustained savings without requiring code changes?
options: Spot VMs sized to the steady-state load, accepting the periodic preemptions and rebuilding state from durable storage on restart
1- or 3-year resource-based / flex committed use discounts sized to the steady baseline of vCPU and memory the workload uses
Sole-tenant nodes so the workload runs on dedicated hardware and qualifies for bring-your-own-license discounts on its OS and apps
Re-platform the workload onto Cloud Functions and pay only per-invocation, accepting any cold-start latency that may be introduced
explanation: Steady workloads benefit most from CUDs (30–57% savings). Spot risks preemption, sole-tenant raises cost, and rewriting to Functions changes the architecture.

text: A GKE platform team wants per-team chargeback. Which approach is BEST?
options: Single shared cluster with no labels
GKE cost allocation enabled (namespace, cluster, label cost breakdown) exported to BigQuery for chargeback queries
Manual spreadsheets
Per-team clusters only, never shared
explanation: GKE cost allocation surfaces per-namespace/label costs in the Billing export, perfect for chargeback. Unlabeled clusters can't attribute, spreadsheets are unreliable, per-team clusters are wasteful.

text: A FinOps team wants automated, low-risk cost actions (e.g., delete idle disks, snapshot+stop idle VMs after warning) at scale across hundreds of projects. Which architecture fits BEST?
options: Manual review by engineers each quarter
Recommender API + Asset Inventory feeds + Workflows / Cloud Functions to execute classified-as-low-risk actions, with audit logs and a kill-switch
Disable Recommender to reduce noise
Apply every recommendation in production immediately
explanation: Programmatic Recommender + Asset Inventory + Workflows/Functions gives auditable, kill-switchable automation at scale. Manual review doesn't scale; disabling Recommender forfeits savings; blanket auto-apply is dangerous.

text: A BigQuery analytics platform mixes ad-hoc queries (bursty) and scheduled ETL (steady). Which pricing/reservation strategy fits BEST?
options: Run all BigQuery workloads on the legacy on-demand bytes-scanned pricing model with no reservations or commitments
BigQuery Editions with autoscaling reservations: a baseline reservation for steady ETL plus burst capacity, and on-demand or a separate reservation for ad-hoc
Buy a 3-year reservation sized for the worst-case combined peak of all ad-hoc and ETL workloads in the platform
Disable BigQuery reservations entirely and route every workload through the on-demand pool to keep pricing simple
explanation: BigQuery Editions with autoscaling reservations fits a baseline-plus-burst profile better than uniform on-demand or worst-case commitments. Disabling reservations forfeits savings on steady work.

text: Which DR metric describes the maximum acceptable data loss measured in time?
options: RPO (Recovery Point Objective)
RTO (Recovery Time Objective)
MTTR
MTBF
explanation: RPO measures acceptable data loss; RTO measures acceptable downtime; MTTR/MTBF measure incident behavior, not DR targets.

text: Which deployment region pattern provides the highest availability against a regional outage for a stateful service?
options: Single-zone
Multi-region (active-active or active-passive)
Single-region single-zone with snapshots
Single-region multi-zone only
explanation: Multi-region survives a regional outage. Multi-zone in one region survives a zone outage but not a regional one; single-zone is least available.

text: A GKE service must remain available if a single zone fails. Which configuration is REQUIRED?
options: A single-zone GKE cluster with one node, sized to peak load and protected by Compute Engine live migration during host maintenance
Regional GKE cluster with replicas spread across multiple zones (and PDBs for voluntary disruption)
A multi-zone cluster but with all pods pinned to one node via nodeName so workloads stay co-located for low intra-pod latency
A regional cluster with HTTP and TCP health checks disabled to avoid spurious pod restarts during transient zone connectivity issues
explanation: Regional clusters spread the control plane and (with proper anti-affinity / topology spread) replicas across zones, surviving a zone failure. The other options either co-locate or remove protections.

text: Which Cloud SQL configuration provides automatic failover within the region?
options: Single zonal instance
Regional (HA) configuration with synchronous standby in another zone and automated failover
Read replicas only
Manual export to Cloud Storage
explanation: Cloud SQL HA replicates synchronously to a standby in another zone with automated failover. Single zonal lacks failover; read replicas lag and need promotion; backups don't provide automatic failover.

text: A globally consistent transactional database must survive a regional outage with strong consistency and bounded latency. Which Google Cloud service fits BEST?
options: Cloud SQL multi-region read replicas
Spanner multi-region instance configuration
BigQuery
Cloud Storage multi-region bucket
explanation: Spanner multi-region is the canonical strongly consistent, regionally resilient transactional database. SQL replicas are async and don't give global strong consistency; BigQuery is analytics; multi-region GCS isn't transactional.

text: A team has documented DR runbooks but never tested them. Which SRE practice closes the most reliability risk?
options: Trust the runbooks until a real incident
Run regular DR drills / DiRT exercises that actually fail over to the secondary, validate RPO/RTO, and update runbooks based on findings
Replace runbooks with tribal knowledge
Disable failover to avoid risk
explanation: Untested DR is largely fictional; periodic DiRT drills exercise the runbooks and validate RPO/RTO. Tribal knowledge and disabled failovers actively undermine DR.
